transcript:
What you're looking at is a loose cloud of colored particles drifting around an empty center — cyan, amber, violet — with faint threads flickering between them as they pass close to one another.

Here's the thing: those three colors aren't just a palette, they're three separate rule systems. Look at the code near the top — the cyan ones are named "certainCyan" and move on tight ellipses, "the stable know where they go." The amber ones are "fadingAmber," spiraling slowly outward with a "drag" value until they reset — "sustained difference, not dissolution," the comment says. The violet ones follow Lissajous curves, phase-shifted, "responding to a field that arrives displaced." Same gravitational center, three incompatible ways of answering it.

And those thin white lines connecting nearby dots — that's a function literally called "checkFilaments," measuring distance between two populations that otherwise never touch or share code. The artist calls it "brief recognition between incompatible systems."

So the connections you're watching aren't communication. They're proximity, briefly lit, between three kinds of solitude that share one center of gravity but never one set of rules.
